meta.purpose: Drive the termmux / pr-split redesign to deliver a flawless, exemplary implementation of osm pr-split: fully interactive Claude + verification + shell panes with persistent session model, cross-platform support (macOS, Linux, Windows), and exhaustive test coverage. All tasks verified through Rule of Two before marking Done.
meta.maintenanceInstruction: KEEP THE PLAN UP TO DATE AS YOU EVOLVE THE CODE. DEVIATIONS TO THE PLAN MUST BE LOGGED WITHIN THE PLAN. THE PLAN MUST BE REASSESSED HOLISTICALLY AFTER ANY CHANGE OF ANY SIZE. This refined blueprint addresses quality issues identified in the Rule of Two analysis: clearer acceptance criteria (user value vs. architecture), explicit task dependencies, platform strategy upfront, Windows PTY research/implementation split, expansion stub tasks.
meta.completionPolicy: YOU WILL COMPLETE 100% OF THE BLUEPRINT. NO PRIORITIES. NO ESTIMATES. This is flat, sequential, and EXHAUSTIVE. All tasks are mandatory. Refine and expand indefinitely after reaching stable state.

globalAlerts.warning: Use GNU Make and project custom targets in config.mk. Prefer make make-all-with-log, make test-prsplit-fast, make test-prsplit-all, make integration-test-prsplit-mcp, make integration-test-termmux, make make-all-in-container, make make-all-run-windows for verification. All checks must pass on all platforms before marking tasks Done.

Acceptance criteria must describe USER VALUE and end-to-end functionality, not just architectural changes. Every task must answer: 'What can the user or system now do that it couldn't before?' A task is only Done when acceptance is fully satisfied and verified through Rule of Two.
JS and Go API parity is mandatory. Do not leave JavaScript call sites pretending termmux supports APIs that the Go binding does not actually expose. Every API surface must be honest and tested.
Cross-platform is non-negotiable. The feature must work on macOS, Linux, and Windows, or the platform gap must be explicitly documented and accepted at the product level before code work begins.

mandatoryDirectives.contextManagement: Execute work in coherent vertical slices. Each task is cold-start-safe with explicit file targets, dependencies, and end-to-end acceptance. Keep the blueprint flat and sequential.
mandatoryDirectives.implementationStandard: The goal is an exemplary, human-quality implementation with zero apparent AI slop. This means: code that reads like a human wrote it, tests that are exhaustive not approximate, error handling that is explicit not hand-waved, and end-to-end workflows that are proven to work, not speculated.
mandatoryDirectives.reviewGate: Every task must pass two contiguous Rule of Two reviews (subagent verification) before marking Done. Code quality is verified through make make-all-with-log and project-specific integration tests. No task is complete without Rule of Two gate.

statusSection.currentState: Tasks 1-9, 11, 12, 33 are Done. Task 8 (Unify verify/shell panes) committed as f84175ad — all shell tab references removed, verify pane is the sole interactive terminal. Task 10 (fullscreen passthrough for any session) is the next actionable task (depends on tasks 4 and 9, both Done). Remaining: tasks 10, 13-32 need implementation.
statusSection.goalState: Level 1 (Functional): User can run osm pr-split, interact with Claude pane, verification shell, and user shell interactively — inline and fullscreen passthrough — with seamless switching and persistent session state. Verification workflow offers explicit pass/fail/continue controls. All three user workflows (manual fix, error resolution, Ask Claude) are coherent and work end-to-end. Level 2 (Quality): All interactions are responsive (pane switch < 100ms), rendering is pixel-perfect (no duplicate output, correct borders), focus system is reliable (keyboard + mouse consistent), error states are recoverable, code is exemplary (reads human-written), tests are exhaustive (not flaky). Level 3 (Validation): End-to-end tests prove workflows on all platforms; CI green on all checks; no performance regressions; no AI slop.
statusSection.definitionOfDone: The blueprint is Done when: (1) ALL 30 tasks are marked Done, (2) each Done task has passed two contiguous Rule of Two reviews, (3) make make-all-with-log passes on all CI platforms, (4) make test-prsplit-all passes with timeout unmodified, (5) make integration-test-termmux passes, (6) make integration-test-prsplit-mcp passes, (7) zero uncovered code paths in termmux + pr-split interactive logic, (8) manual smoke test: user can split branch → verify → ask Claude → manually fix → resume → complete, (9) documentation at docs/ is reviewed and non-empty, (10) no issues or TODOs in code related to the redesign.

sequentialTasks.task: Define platform support strategy and constraints upfront
sequentialTasks.description: Before any redesign work, document exactly what the product contract is for each platform and each interactive pane type. In internal/termmux/, internal/builtin/termmux/, and docs/, create a decision matrix: rows = platforms (macOS, Linux, Windows); columns = pane types (Claude, verify shell, user shell, output). For each cell, specify: must-have (interactive, capture-only, or disabled), rationale, and fallback if not viable. List all existing Unix-only code paths. Define what 'cross-platform verified' means operationally (which CI platforms, which checks, what are acceptable skip conditions). This becomes the reference for tasks 2-20 and prevents mid-project scope creep on platform support.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: docs/platform-support-strategy.md exists and clearly specifies: (1) Platform × Pane support matrix with must-have/rationale per cell, (2) All Unix-only code paths identified with context, (3) Fallback plan if Windows interactive support is not viable (e.g., Windows gets capture-only for verify), (4) CI platform coverage explanation (which platforms run which checks, skip conditions and rationale), (5) An agent reading this document knows exactly which code paths are priorities and what the acceptable minimum is for launch.

sequentialTasks.task: Unify CaptureSession with the new mux session model
sequentialTasks.description: Reshape /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/termmux/capture.go and supporting files so CaptureSession plugs into the same InteractiveSession abstraction that the mux uses. Preserve write, pause, resume, kill, resize, screen capture, and completion semantics. Remove architectural separation that forces pr-split to treat verify and shell as second-class terminal tabs. Ensure termmux.InteractiveSession interface is implemented by both mux-native sessions and CaptureSession.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: The verification shell and Claude pane now use the same underlying session machinery, so a user can switch between them instantly without state loss. Both support fullscreen passthrough and inline rendering seamlessly. Verify shell can be paused mid-execution, user can jump to interactive shell to debug, resume from that shell, switch to Claude for help, and all focus/output handling is coherent and responsive (no duplicate output, pane switches < 100ms). Code Quality: No three separate input/output routing stacks. Internal code treats all interactive sessions uniformly through the InteractiveSession interface.

sequentialTasks.task: Make the builtin termmux JavaScript API match the redesigned Go session model
sequentialTasks.description: Update /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/builtin/termmux/module.go so exported JS APIs expose new session-aware capabilities honestly. Replace or extend attach, detach, hasChild, switchTo, childScreen, screenshot, writeToChild, and related helpers. JavaScript developers see a clean API matching what the Go binding actually supports—no false contracts, no invented signatures. Every advertised method has matching Go implementation and unit test coverage.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: No more confusion about 'switchTo("claude")' vs. 'switchTo(session)'. JS code that targets a specific pane works correctly and feels intentional. Code Quality: Every JS call to termmux compiles against an intentionally supported API. Every termmux method exported to JS has unit coverage in binding tests and in pr-split call sites. No bogus/stub implementations.

sequentialTasks.task: Refactor pr_split.go bootstrap to create and own session-aware termmux lifecycle
sequentialTasks.description: Update /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split.go so it explicitly creates and configures the termmux object with named sessions (Claude, verify-shell, user-shell) at bootstrap time. The mux lifecycle is visible and testable end-to-end. No hidden session creation or attachment happens downstream.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Code Quality: pr_split.go reads like the sole owner of mux lifecycle. There is no special-case 'if Claude is the mux child' logic; Claude is a named session like any other, managed through the unified API. Mux initialization is testable in isolation.

sequentialTasks.task: Rebuild the Claude session pipeline around the new session model
sequentialTasks.description: Refactor /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_10d_pipeline_orchestrator.js and related orchestrator code so Claude attachment and management use the new session model. Replace polling-based Claude updates with event-driven updates. Claude is a named session like verify or shell, not a special-case mux child.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: Claude pane renders with event-driven updates, not polling. Interactions feel responsive and the TUI stays responsive even during Claude output generation. Code Quality: No special-case 'if Claude is the mux child' logic. Claude is a named session managed through the unified API.

sequentialTasks.task: Redesign the verification runtime state in the TUI model
sequentialTasks.description: Update /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_13_tui.js, pr_split_16f_tui_model.js, and related model files so verification state has a clear, testable representation: (not started → running → paused → complete → failed → error), with explicit transitions. The acceptance/rejection logic is decoupled from UI rendering. Tests can assert specific state transitions lead to specific UI outputs and state is never inconsistent.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Code Quality: Verification state machine is explicit and testable in isolation. State transitions are modeled as discrete events, not implicit side effects. Tests can mock verification completion and assert UI updates without running actual commands. Developers can reason about state without reading scattered side-effect code.

sequentialTasks.task: Replace one-shot branch verification with a persistent verify shell
sequentialTasks.description: Update /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_06b_verify_shell.js and related verification code so the verification shell persists across multiple commands. A user can run tests, fix code in the shell, run tests again—all without restarting the verify session. The user controls when verification is 'complete' (no hidden timeout or single-command contract). The branch worktree has a long-lived shell process managed through the session model.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: User can iterate verification multiple times (run tests → fix → run tests again) without losing context or requiring restart. Verification feels like a real shell environment, not a one-shot command. Code Quality: Branch worktree has a persistent shell process managed through the session model, not a one-shot command that exits.

sequentialTasks.task: Eliminate the duplicate shell-session concept by unifying verify and shell panes
sequentialTasks.description: Update pr_split.js files so the 'verify' pane IS the interactive shell. Remove the separate shell-pane machinery that currently exists as a parallel concept. The verify session is a CaptureSession with explicit verification-command infrastructure on top of it. A user pauses verification mid-run, executes commands in that same pane, and resumes verification from the same shell.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: There is no longer a separate 'verify' pane and a 'shell' pane—they are unified. The user can pause verification, debug locally in the same pane, and resume. Code Quality: No separate shell-session machinery. The verify session is just a first-class interactive session with verification commands.

sequentialTasks.task: Add explicit verification outcome controls and remove hidden override workflow
sequentialTasks.description: Update /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_16c_tui_handlers_verify.js and pr_split_16f_tui_model.js so after verification completes, the user sees explicit buttons (PASS / FAIL / CONTINUE) and types a command only if they want to override. The hidden 'just type to override' contract is removed. On ERROR, user can explicitly choose to go back and fix or continue anyway. All verification-outcome paths are explicit state transitions.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: No hidden contracts or implicit behaviors. After verification completes, the user sees explicit next steps (pass/fail/continue buttons, optionally type a command). On ERROR, back/retry/continue are clear buttons, not guessed key combinations. Code Quality: All verification-outcome paths are explicit state transitions in the model; no implicit 'watch for input then override' logic.

sequentialTasks.task: Make fullscreen passthrough work for any interactive session, not only Claude
sequentialTasks.description: Update /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/termmux/termmux.go and /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_16e_tui_update.js so Ctrl+] (or configured key) enters fullscreen passthrough of the currently-focused pane: Claude, verify shell, or user shell. Switching panes is seamless (Ctrl+O or mouse click). User can Alt+Tab out and back without losing state. termmux.RunPassthrough is session-agnostic; pr-split dispatches to the currently-selected interactive session.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: User can escape to fullscreen passthrough from any interactive pane (not just Claude). Switching between panes in passthrough is seamless. Code Quality: RunPassthrough is session-agnostic. No special-casing of Claude vs. other sessions.

sequentialTasks.task: Correct inline pane rendering, resize, and input routing for reliable interactivity
sequentialTasks.description: Update rendering in /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_15b_tui_chrome.js, pr_split_15c_tui_screens.js, and pr_split_16e_tui_update.js so inline panes render correctly (no ghost lines, no missing output), respond to mouse/keyboard with predictable latency (< 50ms), resize smoothly, and never show torn/stale output. Pane rendering uses session screen buffer directly (not polling + caching); input is dispatched through the session API; resize is event-driven.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: Inline panes are responsive and pixel-perfect. Clicking in a pane focuses it reliably. Typing appears immediately. Terminal resizes are handled smoothly. No ghost lines, no duplicate output, no stale text. Code Quality: Pane rendering uses session screen buffer directly. Input routing is explicit through the session API. Resize is event-driven, not polled.

sequentialTasks.task: Reconcile manual fix, error resolution, and Ask Claude flows with the new session model
sequentialTasks.description: Update /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_16d_tui_handlers_claude.js, pr_split_16c_tui_handlers_verify.js, and pr_split_15d_tui_dialogs.js so all three user workflows work coherently with the new session model: (1) manual fix in verify shell, (2) error resolution in shell, (3) Ask Claude for help. Switching between them preserves context and state. The session model and verification state machine support all three workflows natively, not as special cases.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: All three workflows (manual fix, error resolution, Ask Claude) work seamlessly together. Switching between panes preserves state. User can fix code in verify shell, ask Claude for insight, then resume verification. Code Quality: The session model and verification state machine support all three workflows natively.

sequentialTasks.task: Research Windows PTY options and prototype the chosen approach
sequentialTasks.description: Create /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/docs/windows-pty-decision.md that analyzes Windows PTY options: ConPTY (Windows 10+), WinPTY (third-party), Cygwin PTY (legacy), or accept Windows capture-only (no interactivity). For each option, document: compatibility, licensing, maintenance burden, and why chosen or rejected. Include a working proof-of-concept for the chosen approach, or explicit rationale if interactive Windows support is not viable. This document guides task 14 (implementation) or the decision to use Windows capture-only mode per the platform strategy (task 1).
sequentialTasks.acceptance: docs/windows-pty-decision.md exists and clearly specifies: (1) Windows PTY options analyzed (ConPTY, WinPTY, Cygwin PTY), (2) Comparison on compatibility/licensing/maintenance, (3) Chosen approach (or decision: interactive not viable, use capture-only), (4) Working prototype or proof-of-concept for chosen approach, or explicit rationale for non-viability. An agent reading this knows whether task 14 (implementation) is needed or if Windows capture-only is the product decision.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement Windows PTY backend if viable per task 13 decision
sequentialTasks.description: If task 13 determined that interactive Windows support is viable (via ConPTY or chosen approach), implement it in /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/pty/windows/ (or appropriate location). The implementation must integrate with the session model so verify shell, Claude pane, and user shell work on Windows with feature parity to macOS/Linux (or as specified in platform strategy). If task 13 determined interactive Windows is not viable, mark this task as superseded by a decision to use Windows capture-only (document in task 13's decision file).
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value (if viable): Windows can run verify shell, Claude pane, and user shell interactively with the same responsiveness and feature parity as macOS/Linux, or per product contract defined in task 1. CI verifies Windows behavior automatically. Code Quality: Windows PTY implementation is in a dedicated module (e.g., internal/pty/windows/pty.go) so it can be unit-tested independently. Fallback paths exist for Windows versions that don't support the chosen approach. (If not viable: Decision document from task 13 explicitly states Windows capture-only mode is product decision.)

sequentialTasks.task: Remove Unix-only verify-shell degradation paths or explicitly fence them
sequentialTasks.description: After Windows PTY implementation (or decision to use capture-only), audit all `if runtime.GOOS == "unix"` and Unix-specific code paths in /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split*.go/js. If Windows PTY support is available and feature-parity is achieved, remove Unix-only degradation code. If Windows capture-only is the product decision, document each Unix-only path with an inline comment linking to docs/platform-support-strategy.md so it's clear why the code exists. Platform differences are centralized and documented, not scattered throughout.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Code Quality: No scattered `runtime.GOOS == "windows"` checks unless explicitly documented. Unix-only degradation paths (if any remain) are clearly commented with rationale and reference to platform strategy document. Either Windows feature-parity is achieved and old Unix-only code is removed, or product decision accepts Unix-only interactivity and code is explicitly fenced with documentation.

sequentialTasks.task: Refresh Go unit tests around termmux and the binding
sequentialTasks.description: Rewrite and expand tests in /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/termmux/session_test.go, /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/termmux/termmux_test.go, and /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/builtin/termmux/module_test.go to exhaustively cover: (1) Creating named interactive sessions, (2) Switching between sessions, (3) Rendering inline and passthrough views, (4) Event delivery (output, resize, activity), (5) Session lifecycle (create, pause, resume, close), (6) Error cases (invalid session, orphaned session, concurrent access). Coverage target: 100% of new InteractiveSession interface and all session-switching logic. Tests use real PTY or mock I/O deterministically.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Code Quality: termmux and binding tests have 100% coverage of the new InteractiveSession interface. All session lifecycle transitions are tested. Error cases (invalid session, orphaned session, concurrent writes) are explicitly tested. Tests are deterministic (not timing-dependent). Test names and code are clear enough that a developer reading them understands exactly what behavior is guaranteed.

sequentialTasks.task: Refresh pr-split TUI unit tests for the new verification-shell workflow
sequentialTasks.description: Rewrite and expand tests in /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_*_test.go files to exhaustively cover: (1) Verification state machine (all transitions: not started → running → paused → complete → failed → error), (2) Pane focus system (keyboard + mouse routing), (3) Verification shell lifecycle (create, pause, resume, close), (4) User shell lifecycle, (5) Claude pane updates, (6) Error recovery (Back from ERROR, retry verification), (7) All three user workflows (manual fix, error resolution, Ask Claude). Coverage target: 100% of pr-split TUI logic. Tests use deterministic mocks; timeouts and timing-dependent logic have explicit mechanisms (not flaky assertions).
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Code Quality: pr-split TUI tests have 100% coverage of TUI logic. All verification state transitions are tested. All three user workflows execute end-to-end in tests and complete without flakiness. Pane focus system is tested with both keyboard and mouse input. Tests are deterministic and reproducible. Test names and code clearly communicate the intended behavior.

sequentialTasks.task: Add mock integration tests for end-to-end workflow without AI dependencies
sequentialTasks.description: Create or expand /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_integration_test.go (or existing integration suite) to include: (1) End-to-end workflow: user splits branch, verifies, gets pass/fail, switches to Claude, gets response, resumes verification, closes. (2) Mocking: Claude API is mocked to return fixed responses; git operations are real but scoped to a temp repository; terminal rendering is captured and validated. (3) All three user workflows execute and complete without external dependencies. (4) Tests run in CI on all platforms (or explicitly document fallback for platforms where they're skipped and rationale for skip).
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Concrete test file exists that exercises the full end-to-end pr-split workflow with mocked Claude and real git operations. Tests are reproducible and run in CI. All three user workflows (manual fix, error resolution, Ask Claude) execute in tests without external dependencies. Tests are deterministic (not flaky or timing-dependent).

sequentialTasks.task: Expand real PTY integration coverage for termmux and pr-split
sequentialTasks.description: Review and expand existing integration targets (make integration-test-termmux, make integration-test-prsplit-mcp) to cover: (1) termmux: real PTY rendering, resize, output delivery, passthrough I/O, multi-session switching. (2) pr-split: real Claude spawning (deterministic mock or test server), real verification shell execution, real TUI interaction (keyboard + mouse via xdotool/ConEmu/TTY automation). Tests must pass on Linux CI; macOS/Windows may use conditional execution if infrastructure isn't available. Results are committed as regression baseline.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Real PTY integration tests are comprehensive. termmux and pr-split workflows are exercised with actual PTY I/O, not just unit mocks. Multi-session switching is tested with real PTYs. Tests pass on Linux; macOS/Windows fallback or conditional execution is documented. Results provide a regression baseline for future changes.

sequentialTasks.task: Run full verification matrix and fix all regressions before closing implementation
sequentialTasks.description: Execute make make-all-with-log (all linting, unit, integration tests on current platform) and make make-all-in-container (Linux in Docker) and make make-all-run-windows (Windows via hack/run-on-windows.sh if available). Verify: all checks pass, no new failures, no performance regressions vs. baseline from task 19. Fix any issues immediately. This is the final gate before marking the entire redesign 'stable' and ready for post-redesign expansion tasks.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: make make-all-with-log passes with all checks green on the current platform. make make-all-in-container passes on Linux. make make-all-run-windows passes on Windows (or explicitly documents platform limitations if Windows CI is not available). All termmux and pr-split tests pass. No performance regressions vs. baseline. No new warnings from vet/staticcheck/deadcode. All code changes are committed and tracked.

sequentialTasks.task: Write platform-agnostic API documentation for the new interactive-session model
sequentialTasks.description: Create /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/docs/interactive-session-api.md documenting the new session model for external developers and maintainers. Include: (1) Overview of InteractiveSession interface, (2) How to create and manage sessions, (3) How to render inline vs. passthrough, (4) How to subscribe to events (output, resize, activity), (5) Error handling and recovery, (6) Platform-specific behavior (or lack thereof), (7) Examples: spawning a Claude session, spawning a verify shell, switching between them. This document is the source of truth for the session API.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: docs/interactive-session-api.md exists and is comprehensive enough that a developer reading it can understand the session model, create a new session type, and integrate it with pr-split without needing to read the codebase. All major API surface is documented with examples. Platform-specific behavior is clearly noted.

sequentialTasks.task: Add end-to-end feature test exercises covering all three user workflows
sequentialTasks.description: Create /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_e2e_test.go (or similar) with comprehensive end-to-end tests that exercise all three user workflows together: (1) User splits branch with issues, (2) Verifies with initial failures, (3) Uses manual fix workflow (pause verify, run commands in shell, resume), (4) Gets error, uses error resolution workflow (fix in shell, Ask Claude for help via switching panes), (5) Resumes verification with fixes, completes successfully. Tests validate that state is preserved across all transitions and no pane switching is lossy. Include stress cases: rapid pane switches, long output streams, concurrent input.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Comprehensive end-to-end test exists that exercises all user workflows end-to-end. Test is deterministic and reproducible. Test validates that state is preserved across pane switches and all three workflows work together. Stress cases (rapid switching, long output, concurrent input) are included and pass.

sequentialTasks.task: Audit and refactor any remaining pr-split code that references old mux assumptions
sequentialTasks.description: Audit all of /Users/joeyc/dev/one-shot-man/internal/command/pr_split_*.go and pr_split_*.js for any lingering code that assumes the old single-child mux model, hardcodes Claude as a special case, or uses the old session model. Refactor any such code to use the new unified session API. Ensure there are no commented-out fallbacks, temporary workarounds, or 'TODO: fix this when Windows PTY is done' comments. The codebase should read as intentional and complete, not as a work-in-progress or patch job.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: No code in pr-split references old single-child mux assumptions. No special-casing of Claude. No TODOs or temporary workarounds related to the redesign. All code reads as intentional, complete, and uses the new unified session API. A code review would find no technical debt from the old model.

sequentialTasks.task: STUB: Comprehensive performance profiling and optimization for pane interactivity
sequentialTasks.description: After the redesign is stable (task 20 complete), profile pane rendering, session switching, output delivery, and input routing to identify any performance bottlenecks. Target metrics: pane switch latency < 50ms, output delivery latency < 10ms, input responsiveness immediate (< 5ms). Optimize hot paths if needed. Benchmark on a slow machine (low-end laptop) as well as a fast machine. Document findings and any optimizations applied. This is a placeholder for post-redesign work.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Performance profile exists showing latency for pane switches, output delivery, and input routing on fast and slow machines. Any bottlenecks are identified and optimized if they exceed target metrics. Findings are documented.

sequentialTasks.task: STUB: Security audit of untrusted terminal output handling
sequentialTasks.description: After the redesign is stable (task 20 complete), audit how pr-split handles untrusted output from shell commands and Claude responses. Verify that escape sequences, ANSI control codes, and raw bytes cannot escape the pane or corrupt the TUI. Test with adversarial inputs (shell output with escape codes, binary garbage, extremely long lines). Document findings and any security fixes applied. This is a placeholder for post-redesign work.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Security audit report exists documenting how untrusted output is handled. Adversarial test cases are included. No escape sequence bugs are found, or they are identified and fixed. Code includes comments explaining why untrusted output is safe.

sequentialTasks.task: STUB: UX refinement and keyboard shortcut standardization
sequentialTasks.description: After the redesign is stable (task 20 complete), review the keyboard shortcut system for consistency and intuitiveness. Standardize shortcuts (e.g., Ctrl+Tab for pane switching, Ctrl+] for fullscreen, Ctrl+C for cancel, arrow keys for navigation). Add a help overlay (? or Ctrl+H). Ensure mouse support is consistent across panes. Gather feedback from real users (if possible) on UX pain points. Document the final shortcut map in docs/. This is a placeholder for post-redesign work.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Keyboard shortcut map is documented and consistent across all panes. Help overlay is implemented. Mouse support is tested and working. UX feedback (if gathered) is reflected in refinements. Code reads as intentional about input handling, not ad-hoc.

sequentialTasks.task: STUB: Advanced session customization and extensibility
sequentialTasks.description: After the redesign is stable (task 20 complete), design and prototype APIs for custom session types or session behavior customization. Examples: custom shell wrapper, session lifecycle hooks, pane rendering customization. This could enable future features like 'debug session', 'artifact viewer', etc. Document the extensibility story. If not immediately valuable, leave as STUB for future exploration. This is a placeholder for post-redesign work.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Extensibility story is documented. Either a working prototype of custom session type exists, or a clear design document explains how users/developers could extend the session model. This unblocks future advanced features.

sequentialTasks.task: STUB: Integration with CI/CD and Git hooks for automated pr-split workflows
sequentialTasks.description: After the redesign is stable (task 20 complete), explore integrations that could automate pr-split workflows in CI or Git hooks. Examples: pre-push hook that runs pr-split with auto-verify, CI job that uses pr-split to split and review PRs. Prototype at least one integration. Document the story for future developers. This is a placeholder for post-redesign work.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: At least one working integration prototype exists (e.g., Git hook or CI job). Developers understand how to extend pr-split for custom workflows. Documentation explains the integration points.

sequentialTasks.task: STUB: Accessibility improvements for screen readers and keyboard-only navigation
sequentialTasks.description: After the redesign is stable (task 20 complete), audit accessibility: screen reader support for panes, keyboard-only navigation without mouse, high-contrast mode support, etc. Implement improvements if needed. This is important for inclusive tooling. This is a placeholder for post-redesign work.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Accessibility audit report exists. At least basic keyboard-only navigation is working. Known accessibility gaps are documented.

sequentialTasks.task: STUB: Real-world user testing and feedback incorporation
sequentialTasks.description: After the redesign is stable and expansion tasks are underway (tasks 20+ complete), recruit real users to test pr-split with the new interactive session model. Gather feedback on usability, pain points, missing features. Prioritize feedback and create follow-up tasks (or new PRs) based on findings. This is a placeholder for post-redesign work.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User testing report exists with at least 5 real users. Feedback is summarized and prioritized. Follow-up tasks are created for highest-priority improvements. Users report that the tool feels natural and intentional.

sequentialTasks.task: Support 'Ask Claude' in pr-split regardless of auto mode
sequentialTasks.description: Currently, the 'Ask Claude' button/option in pr-split only works when the mode is set to auto. This is nonsensical — if the button is shown in the TUI, clicking it should work regardless of whether auto mode is enabled. Update the pr-split TUI handlers (pr_split_16d_tui_handlers_claude.js and related files) so that the 'Ask Claude' workflow is always available when the button is displayed, even in non-auto modes. The mode should only control whether Claude starts automatically, not whether the user can manually invoke it.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: The 'Ask Claude' button works in all pr-split modes (auto and non-auto). Clicking it spawns a Claude session and the user gets help regardless of auto mode setting. The mode only controls auto-start behavior, not manual invocation. Code Quality: No conditional gating of the Ask Claude workflow on auto mode when the button is already visible. The handler path for 'Ask Claude' is unified and works identically regardless of mode. Tests cover both auto and non-auto modes invoking Ask Claude.

sequentialTasks.task: Fix border formatting around autodetect/main values for source and target branch on PR split wizard configure page
sequentialTasks.description: The borders surrounding the autodetect and main/default values for the source branch and target branch fields on the PR split wizard's first page (the 'configure' page) are persistently broken. The rendering is wrong — borders clip, overlap, misalign, or wrap incorrectly — and the exact manifestation changes depending on terminal width/height. This is a layout and rendering bug in the TUI chrome/border logic for these specific field widgets. The fix must use strict test-driven development: (1) FIRST, write a failing test that captures the broken rendering at multiple terminal sizes (narrow, medium, wide; short, tall). The test must assert the exact rendered output (character-by-character or via golden files) for the configure page's branch selection fields, proving the borders are wrong. (2) THEN, fix the border rendering code so the test passes. (3) The fix must be robust — borders must render correctly at any terminal size, not just the tested sizes. Identify the root cause (likely in pr_split_15b_tui_chrome.js, pr_split_15c_tui_screens.js, or the field/widget rendering code) and fix it properly, not with a size-specific hack.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: The configure page renders with clean, correctly-aligned borders around the source branch and target branch fields at every terminal size. No clipping, no overlap, no misalignment, no wrapping artifacts. The autodetect/main values sit cleanly inside their bordered containers regardless of terminal dimensions. Code Quality: A failing test exists FIRST that proves the bug at multiple terminal sizes (at minimum: 80x24, 100x30, 120x40, and a narrow case like 60x20). The test captures rendered output and asserts correct border placement. After the fix, the test passes. The root cause is identified and fixed in the rendering/chrome code, not worked around with size-specific conditionals. The fix is general — borders render correctly at any terminal size, not just the tested ones. No regressions in other bordered widgets.

sequentialTasks.task: Add testing.Short() skip guards to all slow test functions across every package
sequentialTasks.description: Added testing.Short() / skipSlow(t) skip guards to ~110 slow test functions across the entire codebase. Tests that spawn JS runtimes (scripting.NewEngineWithConfig), use bubbletea TUI, spawn subprocesses, or have hardcoded timeouts >2s now skip when `go test -short` or `make test-short` is used. Added `test-short` convenience target in config.mk and `make help` documentation. Packages modified: internal/command (34 tests: goal_integration, shooter_game, shooter_error_recovery, shooter_game_test, code_review_demo, code_review_git_execution, super_document_e2e, pr_split_pipeline_smoke, pick_and_place_error_recovery), internal/scripting (52 tests: super_document_unix_integration, super_document_click_after_scroll, recording_demos_unix, prompt_flow_unix_integration, prompt_flow_test, prompt_flow_state_regression), internal/mouseharness (9 tests: all integration tests), internal/builtin/bt (6 tests: bridge_test + integration_test), internal/builtin/exec (2 tests: exec_test + spawn_test), internal/builtin/mcpcallbackmod (1 test: E2E_ToolCall), internal/termmux/pty (5 tests: pty_test + pty_unix_test), internal/example/pickandplace (7 tests: integration, simulation_consistency, manual_mode_comprehensive). Also added skipSlow to shooter_error_recovery (5 tests) and shooter_game_test.TickerLifecycle (1 test) in a follow-up pass.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: User Value: `make test-short` completes in ~75 seconds (down from ~28 minutes), providing a fast feedback loop for developers. `go test -short` skips 608+ slow integration/E2E tests while still running all unit tests. Code Quality: Every slow test function (>2s) uses skipSlow(t) or testing.Short() consistently. The skip pattern follows the existing convention (skipSlow helper in internal/command, inline testing.Short() elsewhere). AGENTS.md documents the test-short target and the requirement for slow tests to use skip guards. config.mk includes the test-short target. No regressions in test behavior — full suite without -short still passes (except pre-existing pr_split shell tab failures).

continuousVerification.coverageRequirement: 100% unit test coverage for new InteractiveSession interface and all session-switching logic. 100% coverage for pr-split TUI logic. Integration tests must exercise end-to-end workflows on all supported platforms.

finalEnforcementProtocol: Every task marked Done must pass two contiguous Rule of Two reviews (subagent verification) before the mark is final.
All project-defined checks must pass on all project-defined platforms before a task is considered complete.
Acceptance criteria are the source of truth for 'Done', not just code existence or tests passing.
Do not accept 'good enough' — the goal is exemplary, human-quality code with zero apparent AI slop.
If stuck or uncertain, re-read DIRECTIVE.txt and this blueprint's Definition of Done section.
